0: <title>Life cycle assessment of Japanese beef-fattening systems using local food by-product feeds </title>

Mikinori TSUIKI Ayami SASAKI*, Tsuneo KONDO**, Masakazu HIGASHIYAMA™ Takayuki MURAMOTO* and Suguru SAIGA Department of Agriculture, Iwate University, Japan National Agricultural Research Center for Tohoku Region, NARO, Japan,
1: Environmental impacts of beef-fattening systems using local food by-product feeds were compared with those using concentrate feeds by a life cycle assessment method. Apple juice pulp and wheat bran were the local food by-product feeds evaluated. Cultivation, transport, processing, animal management, digestion process and waste management were used for estimations of global warming potential, acidification potential and eutrophication potential. Economic worth was used to allocate the environmental burden between juice and pulp and between flour and bran. The functional units for feed production and beef cattle fattening were 1 metric ton of total digestible nutrients and 1 kg of body weight gain, respectively. Contributions to global warming and acidification were high in the concentrate feeds beef-fattening system. Fossil fuel consumption by overseas transport influenced these impacts. Fossil fuel consumption during drying of apple juice pulp enhanced the global warming and acidification effects. As manure was excessively applied for grass and apple cultivation, contributions of grass silage and apple juice pulp to eutrophication were high. The use of local food by-products as feeds reduced global warming and acidification potential. The application of optimum amounts of chemical fertilizers and manure is important for reducing eutrophication potential.
Key Words: Beef-fattening, Food by-products, Life cycle assessment, Local feed

3: Japanese consumers have recently become more aware of food safety and the environmental impacts of food production. In 2007, 1.6 million head of dairy cattle and 2.7 million head of beef cattle were kept in Japan, and average number of cattle kept on each farm has increased rapidly even now (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries 2007a). This rapid growth was sustained by imported feeds. The surplus manure resulting from this intensification of livestock farming causes severe pollution problems.
Concentrate feeds are generally used for beef fattening in Japan, the main components of which are

imported corn, barley and wheat bran. The environ- mental impact of these imported feeds needs to be compared with that of self-supplied feeds, with a view to establishing and promoting more environ- mentally friendly livestock production systems.

Iwate Prefecture is one of the growing districts of apple and wheat in Japan. Apple juice pulp and wheat bran are produced by apple juice and milling companies. The effective utilization of these local food by-products as feed is important to reduce industrial wastes and to increase feed self-sufficiency.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a useful method of comparing the environmental impacts of various types of livestock production systems. LCA has been applied to milk production (Masoni et al. 1998, Halberg 1999, Cederberg and Mattsson 2000, Haas et al. 2001, Iepema and Pijnenburg 2001, Eide 2002, Cederberg and

Stadig 2003, De Boer 2003, Hospido et al. 2003, Cederberg and Flysjo 2004, Basset-Mens et al. 2005, Chen et al. 2005, Casey and Holden 2005, Masuda et al. 2005, Basset-Mens et al. 2007, Thomassen et al. 2008, Tsuiki et al. 2009), beef production (Kumm 2002, Ogino et al. 2004; 2007, Nunez et al. 2005, Casey and Holden 2006), pork production (Halberg 1999, Kumm 2002, Nunez et al. 2005, Van der Werf et al. 2005, Basset-Mens and Van der Werf 2005), lamb production (Kumm 2002) and ostrich production (Nunez et al. 2005). Several papers have studied organic production systems (Cederberg and Mattsson 2000, De Boer 2003, Haas et al. 2001, Kumm 2002).
In this study, environmental impacts of Japanese beef-fattening system with local food by-product feeds were compared with those using concentrate feeds by a LCA method.

6: The objective of this study was to compare, by the LCA method, the environmental impacts of Japanese beef- fattening systems using two types of feeds, locally produced food by-product feeds and imported concentrate feeds. Apple juice pulp and domestic wheat bran, which were important food by-products in Iwate Prefecture were evaluated. The functional unit for feed production and beef cattle production was 1 metric ton of total digestible nutrients (TDN) of feed (National Agricultural Research Organization 2001) and 1 kg of body weight gain of cattle during the fattening period.
CO2, CH4, N2O, SO2, NOx, NHs, NO3
Fig. 1 The system boundary of beef-fattening system

The system boundaries are defined in Fig. 1. Cultivation, transport, processing, animal management, digestion process and waste management were considered in the estimations of global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (AP) and eutrophication potential (EP). The production processes of chemical fertilizers and pesticides were contained in this system. Environmental impacts of by-product feeds were allocated according to their prices. The ratio of allocation was 625:32 for juice and pulp, and 270:28 for flour and bran.

The feeding conditions of two beef-fattening systems are shown in Table 1. Eight Japanese Shorthorn steers were fed at the National Agricultural Research Center for Tohoku Region in 2003. Feeding period was 2.2 months shorter in the concentrate feed plot than in the by-product feed plot. Average daily liveweight gains on the concentrate feed plot and by-product feed plot were 0.92 and 0.80 kg head day-1, respectively. 9: <header>2.2 Life Cycle Inventories </header>

Carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N20), sulfur dioxide (SO2), nitrogen oxide (NOx), ammonia (NH3), and nitrate (NO3) were taken into account. Material consumptions and output materials associated with feed production and fattening were shown in Table 2 and 3. The energy and material utilization during the production processes of corn, barley and wheat in the USA were evaluated using data by Pimentel (1980). Domestic wheat bran and apple juice pulp were evaluated as local food by-product feeds. The energy and material utilization of the production processes of these feeds and grass silage were evaluated using typical production standard of wheat, apple and grass (Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries Technical Information Society 1997). Manure was applied during the production of grass and apples. Consumption of fossil fuels and electricity for the processing of apple juice was evaluated (Kikkoman Inc. 2004). Waste treatment was evaluated using the emission factors by Husted (1993; 1994) and Haga (2002). 1.05% of waste organic matter carbon (C) was converted to CH4-C

(Husted 1993; 1994). 0.75% and 10% of waste nitrogen (N) was converted to N2O-N and NH3-N, respectively (Haga 2002). If manure was applied for cultivation of feeds, emissions of waste treatment was set off to avoid double counting of emissions. The amount of CH4 emissions from the cattle rumen was estimated from dry matter intake (Shibata et al. 1993). The amount of NO3 was estimated from farm gate balance of N, assuming that surplus N eluviated as NO.

If manure was applied for cultivation of feeds, emissions of waste treatment was set off to avoid double counting of emissions. 11: Emissions of each material were allocated to different impact categories according to their potential environ- mental impacts. GWP, AP and EP were selected as the environmental impact categories. The unit of GWP was CO2 equivalent. The ratios of GWP values were 1:23:296 for CO2, CH4 and N20, respectively (IPCC 2001). The unit of AP was SO2 equivalent. The ratios of AP values were 1.00:0.70:1.88 for SO2, NO and NH3, respectively (Heijungs et al. 1992). The unit of EP was PO4 equivalent. 3- The ratios of EP values were 1.00:0.10:0.35 for PO43- NO3 and NH3, respectively (Heijungs et al. 1992). As most of PO43 in underground water is adsorbed by soil in Japanese environment, only NO3 and NH3 were evaluated for EP.

1: GWP of six feeds were shown in Fig. 2. The total contributions to GWP were 944, 817, 279, 1,008, 1,189 and 1,136 kgCO2 eq. tTDN for grass silage, apple juice pulp, domestic wheat bran, imported wheat bran, corn and barley, respectively (Fig. 2). GWP of imported feeds (imported wheat bran, corn and barley) was higher than domestic feeds. Domestic wheat bran showed lowest GWP.

The contributions (%) of each feed production process to GWP were shown in Fig.3. The proportions of CO2 on imported wheat bran, corn and barley were 86.4%, 83.3% and 90.2%, respectively, because of the high fossil fuel consumption of imported feed transport (Figs. 2 and 3). The drying process of apple juice pulp using fossil fuels increased GWP (Figs. 2 and 3). As N2O and CH4 were emitted during composting and from cultivated fields after manure application, GWP of grass silage and apple juice pulp were higher than that of domestic wheat bran.

1: Local food by-products are important feed resources. If they are not used as feed, they must be disposed of, possibly incurring additional environmental impacts. Moreover, the quality of some local by-products is equal to that of concentrates. Thus, the use of local food by- products avoids the use of imported feeds, improves feed

Iwate Prefecture is one of the growing districts of apple and wheat in Japan. Apple juice pulp and wheat bran are produced by apple juice and milling companies. The effective utilization of these local food by-products as feed is important to reduce industrial wastes and to increase feed self-sufficiency. So in this study, apple juice pulp and domestic wheat bran were evaluated as local by-product feeds.

This study showed that fossil fuel consumption by transport and drying of feed contributed substantially to GWP and AP. The use of self-supplied feeds and domestically produced feeds therefore has an important role in reducing GWP and AP values. Van der Werf et al. (2005) showed that for imported feeds (cassava chips, cane molasses, soy oil, soy cake and sunflower cake), sea and road transport accounted for about 200 kg CO2 eq. t-FreshWeight`1. This value is lower than that in our study (513~666 kg CO2 eq. tTDN-1, calculated from Figs. 2 and 3) for two possible reasons. First, overseas transport in the Van der Werf et al. (2005) was from Brazil to France, which is shorter than in our this study, from the East Coast of USA to Japan. Second, TDN was used in our study as a functional unit of LCA, which was a part of the fresh weight used by Van der Werf et al. (2005). If the same amount of environmental impact was evaluated, impact per TDN shows higher value than per fresh weight.

NH3, CH4 and N20 are emitted during composting, and N2O is emitted from cultivated fields after manure application. Therefore manure management is important for GWP and AP. Many kinds of composting methods are used in Japan, and emission factors of these methods vary widely (Ministry of the Environment 2008). In future, greater use of composting methods that have low emission factors is necessary to reduce GWP and AP.

EP of grass silage and apple juice pulp was high as a result of large application of nitrogen (grass silage: 400 kg N ha-1, apple juice pulp: 530 kg N ha-1 by chemical fertilizers and manure. But it will be possible to diminish EP without reducing productivity by keeping application standard of nutrients (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries 2007b).
